questions.stem: A continuous surjection $p : E \to B$ is a covering map if every point of $B$ has an open neighborhood $U$ such that:
options: $U$ is homeomorphic to an open ball.
$p^{-1}(U)$ is a disjoint union of open sets, each mapped homeomorphically onto $U$ by $p$.
questions.explanation: By definition, $U$ is evenly covered: $p^{-1}(U)$ is a disjoint union of open sets (the sheets), each carried homeomorphically onto $U$ by $p$. This local-triviality is the heart of the covering space definition.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: An open set $U \subseteq B$ over which $p^{-1}(U)$ splits into sheets each mapped homeomorphically to $U$ is called:
questions.explanation: Such a $U$ is called an evenly covered neighborhood, and the disjoint open pieces of $p^{-1}(U)$ are the sheets over $U$. A covering map requires every point of the base to have one.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The standard covering of the circle by the real line is given by:
options: $p(t) = (\cos t, \sin t)$ with $t \in \mathbb{R}$, i.e. $p(t) = e^{2\pi i t}$ after rescaling.
questions.explanation: The exponential map $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, $p(t) = e^{2\pi i t} = (\cos 2\pi t, \sin 2\pi t)$, is the prototypical covering map. It wraps the line around the circle infinitely often.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: For the covering $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, $p(t) = e^{2\pi i t}$, the fiber $p^{-1}(\{1\})$ over the point $1 \in S^1$ is:
questions.explanation: $p(t) = 1$ exactly when $2\pi t$ is a multiple of $2\pi$, i.e. when $t \in \mathbb{Z}$. So the fiber over $1$ is $\mathbb{Z}$, an infinite discrete set, reflecting the infinitely many sheets.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The covering $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$ has how many sheets?
questions.explanation: Each fiber of $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$ is a coset of $\mathbb{Z}$, hence countably infinite. The number of sheets equals the cardinality of a fiber, which is countably infinite.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: For a covering $p : E \to B$ with $B$ connected, the number of sheets equals:
options: the cardinality of the fiber $p^{-1}(b)$, which is the same for every $b \in B$.
questions.explanation: When $B$ is connected, all fibers have the same cardinality, called the number of sheets (or degree) of the covering. The function $b \mapsto |p^{-1}(b)|$ is locally constant, hence constant on a connected base.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The map $p : S^1 \to S^1$, $p(z) = z^n$ (for fixed integer $n \ge 1$), is a covering map with:
questions.explanation: The map $z \mapsto z^n$ wraps the circle around itself $n$ times; each point has exactly $n$ preimages (the $n$-th roots). So it is an $n$-sheeted covering of the circle by itself.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The path lifting property states that for a covering $p : E \to B$, a path $f : [0,1] \to B$ and a point $e_0 \in p^{-1}(f(0))$:
options: there is a lift only if $f$ is a loop.
there are infinitely many lifts starting at $e_0$.
there is a unique path $\tilde{f} : [0,1] \to E$ with $p \circ \tilde{f} = f$ and $\tilde{f}(0) = e_0$.
questions.explanation: Path lifting: given a starting point $e_0$ above $f(0)$, there is a unique lift $\tilde{f}$ of $f$ with $\tilde{f}(0) = e_0$. Uniqueness comes from the covering's local-homeomorphism structure and connectedness of $[0,1]$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The homotopy lifting property for a covering $p : E \to B$ guarantees that a homotopy $F : [0,1] \times [0,1] \to B$, once a lift of its initial stage is chosen:
options: lifts uniquely to a homotopy $\tilde{F}$ in $E$.
lifts only if $B$ is simply connected.
questions.explanation: Covering maps have the homotopy lifting property: any homotopy in $B$ lifts uniquely once a lift of the starting map is fixed. This is what makes endpoints of lifts homotopy invariants and underlies the computation of $\pi_1(S^1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A key consequence of homotopy lifting is that if two paths in $B$ are path-homotopic, then their lifts starting at the same point:
options: have the same starting point but possibly different endpoints.
are path-homotopic and in particular end at the same point.
questions.explanation: Path-homotopic paths in $B$ lift (from a common start) to path-homotopic paths in $E$; in particular their lifts share the same endpoint. This monodromy fact is the engine of the fundamental group computation for the circle.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Using $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, the loop $\omega(s) = e^{2\pi i s}$ based at $1$ lifts to the path in $\mathbb{R}$ starting at $0$ given by:
questions.explanation: We need $p(\tilde{\omega}(s)) = e^{2\pi i s}$ with $\tilde{\omega}(0) = 0$; the lift is $\tilde{\omega}(s) = s$, ending at $\tilde{\omega}(1) = 1 \in \mathbb{Z}$. The endpoint $1$ is the winding number of the loop.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The loop $\omega^n(s) = e^{2\pi i n s}$ in $S^1$ (winding $n$ times) lifts, starting at $0$, to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ ending at:
questions.explanation: The lift of $e^{2\pi i n s}$ starting at $0$ is $\tilde{\omega}(s) = n s$, which ends at $n$. The endpoint of the lift, an integer, is exactly the winding number, giving the isomorphism $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The lifting correspondence sending a loop class in $\pi_1(S^1, 1)$ to the endpoint of its lift starting at $0$ defines an isomorphism:
questions.explanation: The endpoint map is a well-defined bijection $\pi_1(S^1,1) \to \mathbb{Z}$ (well-defined by homotopy lifting), and it is a homomorphism, hence an isomorphism $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A deck transformation (covering transformation) of $p : E \to B$ is:
options: a homeomorphism $B \to B$ fixing the basepoint.
a homeomorphism $h : E \to E$ with $p \circ h = p$.
questions.explanation: A deck transformation is a self-homeomorphism $h$ of the total space $E$ that commutes with the projection: $p \circ h = p$. Such maps permute each fiber and form a group under composition.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: For the covering $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$, $p(t) = e^{2\pi i t}$, the group of deck transformations is:
questions.explanation: The deck transformations are exactly the integer translations $t \mapsto t + n$, since these are the homeomorphisms of $\mathbb{R}$ commuting with $p$. They form a group isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}$, matching $\pi_1(S^1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A covering space $E$ of $B$ is called the universal cover when:
questions.explanation: The universal cover is the (connected) covering space that is simply connected. It covers every other connected covering of $B$, which is why it is called universal. For example $\mathbb{R}$ is the universal cover of $S^1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The universal cover of the circle $S^1$ is:
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{R}$ is simply connected and covers $S^1$ via $p(t) = e^{2\pi i t}$, so it is the universal cover of the circle. The deck group $\mathbb{Z}$ recovers $\pi_1(S^1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The universal cover of the torus $T^2 = S^1 \times S^1$ is:
questions.explanation: Since $\mathbb{R}$ covers each circle factor, $\mathbb{R}^2 = \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}$ covers $T^2$, and $\mathbb{R}^2$ is simply connected. The deck group is $\mathbb{Z}^2 = \pi_1(T^2)$, acting by integer translations.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The double cover $p : S^2 \to \mathbb{RP}^2$ (identifying antipodal points) is the universal cover of $\mathbb{RP}^2$ because:
options: $S^2$ is simply connected, so it is the universal cover, and the deck group $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$.
questions.explanation: $S^2$ is simply connected and double-covers $\mathbb{RP}^2$, hence is its universal cover. The deck group is $\{\pm 1\} \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$, which equals $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Given a covering $p : (E, e_0) \to (B, b_0)$, the induced map $p_* : \pi_1(E, e_0) \to \pi_1(B, b_0)$ is:
options: injective, so $\pi_1(E)$ embeds as a subgroup of $\pi_1(B)$.
questions.explanation: For a covering map, $p_*$ is injective (a loop in $B$ whose lift is a loop and is null-homotopic comes from a null-homotopic loop). Thus $\pi_1(E)$ is realized as a subgroup of $\pi_1(B)$, the basis of the Galois correspondence.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Galois correspondence for covering spaces relates connected covers of a (nice) space $B$ to:
options: conjugacy classes of subgroups of $\pi_1(B)$ (subgroups, up to base point).
questions.explanation: Connected covers of $B$ correspond to subgroups of $\pi_1(B)$ (based covers to actual subgroups via $p_*\pi_1(E)$; unbased covers to conjugacy classes). The universal cover corresponds to the trivial subgroup.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Under the correspondence between covers of $B$ and subgroups of $\pi_1(B)$, the number of sheets of the cover associated to a subgroup $H \le \pi_1(B)$ equals:
questions.explanation: The number of sheets of the cover corresponding to $H$ is the index $[\pi_1(B):H]$. For the universal cover, $H$ is trivial, so the number of sheets is $|\pi_1(B)|$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The universal cover of a space $B$ with $\pi_1(B) \cong \mathbb{Z}/5$ has how many sheets?
questions.explanation: The universal cover corresponds to the trivial subgroup, so its number of sheets is the index of the trivial subgroup in $\pi_1(B)$, namely $|\pi_1(B)| = |\mathbb{Z}/5| = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space $B$ admits a simply connected covering space (a universal cover) provided $B$ is path-connected, locally path-connected, and:
questions.explanation: The existence theorem requires $B$ to be path-connected, locally path-connected, and semilocally simply connected; the last condition (every point has a neighborhood in which loops are null-homotopic in $B$) is exactly what is needed to build the universal cover.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Analyze the exponential covering of the circle.
questions.solution: Consider $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$ given by $p(t) = e^{2\pi i t}$. The loop $\omega(s) = e^{2\pi i s}$ lifted from the starting point $0$ ends at the point {{1}} in $\mathbb{R}$. The number of sheets of this covering is {{2}}, since each fiber is a copy of the integers. The lifting correspondence gives $\pi_1(S^1) \cong$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The lift of $\omega$ from $0$ is $\tilde{\omega}(s) = s$, ending at $1$. Each fiber is a coset of $\mathbb{Z}$, so the covering is countably-infinitely sheeted, and the endpoint map yields $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Count sheets for power maps of the circle.
questions.solution: The map $p : S^1 \to S^1$ given by $p(z) = z^3$ is a covering with {{1}} sheets. More generally $z \mapsto z^n$ has $n$ sheets, and the number of preimages of any single point under $z \mapsto z^6$ is {{2}}. The total space $S^1$ of these finite covers is {{3}} simply connected.
questions.explanation: $z \mapsto z^n$ has exactly $n$ preimages over each point, so $z^3$ gives $3$ sheets and $z^6$ gives $6$ preimages. The circle has $\pi_1 \cong \mathbb{Z} \ne 1$, so it is not simply connected (these are not the universal cover).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Relate the universal cover to the order of the fundamental group.
questions.solution: The double cover $S^2 \to \mathbb{RP}^2$ has simply connected total space, so it is the universal cover and its number of sheets is {{1}}. Consequently $|\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)|$ equals {{2}}, and the group $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The universal cover's sheet count equals the order of $\pi_1$. The double cover has $2$ sheets, so $|\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2)| = 2$ and $\pi_1(\mathbb{RP}^2) \cong \mathbb{Z}/2$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Use the index to count sheets of a cover.
questions.solution: Let $B$ have $\pi_1(B) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, and let $H = 4\mathbb{Z} \le \mathbb{Z}$. The connected cover corresponding to $H$ has number of sheets equal to the index $[\mathbb{Z} : 4\mathbb{Z}]$, which is {{1}}. The deck transformation group of $p : \mathbb{R} \to S^1$ is generated by the translation $t \mapsto t + 1$, so the smallest positive translation amount that is a deck transformation is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The index $[\mathbb{Z}:4\mathbb{Z}] = 4$, so that cover has $4$ sheets. The deck group of $\mathbb{R} \to S^1$ is the integer translations, whose smallest positive generator translates by $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
